parentQuestion: What type of cells line the ventricles of the brain?

scaffolds.q: The brain contains fluid-filled cavities called ventricles.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Four ventricles (two lateral, one third, one fourth) are connected chambers filled with cerebrospinal fluid.

scaffolds.q: Glial cells are support cells in the nervous system - they're not neurons.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Glial cells (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, ependymal cells) provide support, nutrition, and protection - they don't transmit electrical signals.

scaffolds.q: Ependymal cells are a specific type of glial cell.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Ependymal cells are classified as glial cells even though they form an epithelial-like lining. They're specialized support cells.

scaffolds.q: Ependymal cells have cilia on their surface.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The cilia beat in coordinated waves, helping circulate cerebrospinal fluid through the ventricular system.

scaffolds.q: CSF is produced by specialized ependymal cells in the choroid plexus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The choroid plexus contains modified ependymal cells that filter blood plasma and actively secrete CSF - about 500mL per day.

scaffolds.q: Neurons do NOT line body cavities - that's a job for epithelial-type cells.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Neurons transmit signals - they don't form barrier layers. The ventricles are lined by ependymal cells (glial, epithelial-like).

scaffolds.q: Astrocytes support neurons but don't line the ventricles.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Astrocytes regulate the blood-brain barrier and support neurons throughout brain tissue, but they don't form the ventricular lining.

scaffolds.q: The cells lining the brain ventricles are called ependymal cells.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Ependymal cells form the ventricular lining. They're ciliated glial cells that help circulate CSF and produce it in the choroid plexus.

scaffolds.q: The answer is EPENDYMAL CELLS - the ciliated glial cells that line the ventricles.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not neurons (they transmit signals, don't line cavities), not astrocytes (they support neurons in tissue, not ventricular lining), not 'epithelial cells' (too vague - ependymal is the specific term).
